parts.prose: 8.3.10 Additional testing procedure for service provider assessments only: If passwords/passphrases are used as the only authentication factor for customer user access to cardholder data, examine guidance provided to customer users to verify that the guidance includes all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Using a password/passphrase as the only authentication factor provides a single point of failure if compromised. Therefore, in these implementations, controls are needed to minimize how long malicious activity could occur via a compromised password/passphrase.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Passwords/passphrases that are valid for a long time without a change provide malicious individuals with more time to break the password/phrase. Periodically changing passwords offers less time for a malicious individual to crack a password/passphrase and less time to use a compromised password.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Where authentication factors such as physical or logical security tokens, smart cards, or certificates are used:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Factors are assigned to an individual user and not shared among multiple users.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Physical and/or logical controls ensure only the intended user can use that factor to gain access.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: An authentication factor cannot be used by anyone other than the user to which it is assigned.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine authentication policies and procedures to verify that procedures for using authentication factors such as physical security tokens, smart cards, and certificates are defined and include all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interview security personnel to verify authentication factors are assigned to an individual user and not shared among multiple users.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine system configuration settings and/or observe physical controls, as applicable, to verify that controls are implemented to ensure only the intended user can use that factor to gain access.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: If multiple users can use authentication factors such as tokens, smart cards, and certificates, it may be impossible to identify the individual using the authentication mechanism.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Having physical and/or logical controls (for example, a PIN, biometric data, or a password) to uniquely authenticate the user of the account will prevent unauthorized users from gaining access to the user account through use of a shared authentication factor.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE for personnel with administrative access.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Administrative access to the CDE cannot be obtained by the use of a single authentication factor. Applicability Notes The requirement for MFA for non-console administrative access applies to all personnel with elevated or increased privileges accessing the CDE via a non-console connection—that is, via logical access occurring over a network interface rather than via a direct, physical connection.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine network and/or system configurations to verify MFA is required for all non- console into the CDE for personnel with administrative access.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe administrator personnel logging into the CDE and verify that MFA is required.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Requiring more than one type of authentication factor reduces the probability that an attacker can gain access to a system by masquerading as a legitimate user, because the attacker would need to compromise multiple authentication factors. This is especially true in environments where traditionally the single authentication factor employed was something a user knows such as a password or passphrase.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Implementing MFA for non-console administrative access to in-scope system components that are not part of the CDE will help prevent unauthorized users from using a single factor to gain access and compromise in-scope system components.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Using one factor twice (for example, using two separate passwords) is not considered multi- factor authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Application or system accounts performing automated functions.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: User accounts on point-of-sale terminals that have access to only one card number at a time to facilitate a single transaction.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: User accounts that are only authenticated with phishing-resistant authentication factors.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Access into the CDE cannot be obtained by the use of a single authentication factor. Applicability Notes This requirement does not apply to: MFA is required for both types of access specified in Requirements 8.4.2 and 8.4.3. Therefore, applying MFA to one type of access does not replace the need to apply another instance of MFA to the other type of access. If an individual first connects to the entity’s network via remote access, and then later initiates a connection into the CDE from within the network, per this requirement the individual would authenticate using MFA twice, once when connecting via remote access to the entity’s network and once when connecting from the entity’s network into the CDE. The MFA requirements apply for all types of system components, including cloud, hosted systems, and on-premises applications, network security devices, workstations, servers, and endpoints, and includes access directly to an entity’s networks or systems as well as web-based access to an application or function. MFA for access into the CDE can be implemented at the network or system/application level; it does not have to be applied at both levels. For example, if MFA is used when a user connects to the CDE network, it does not have to be used when the user logs into each system or application within the CDE. This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine network and/or system configurations to verify MFA is implemented for all non-console access into the CDE.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe personnel logging in to the CDE and examine evidence to verify that MFA is required.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Requiring more than one type of authentication factor reduces the probability that an attacker can gain access to a system by masquerading as a legitimate user, because the attacker would need to compromise multiple authentication factors. This is especially true in environments where traditionally the single authentication factor employed was something a user knows such as a password or passphrase.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Using one factor twice (for example, using two separate passwords) is not considered multi- factor authentication.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Refer to Appendix G for the definition of “phishing resistant authentication.”

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: MFA is implemented for all remote access originating from outside the entity’s network that could access or impact the CDE.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Remote access to the entity’s network cannot be obtained by using a single authentication factor. Applicability Notes The requirement for MFA for remote access originating from outside the entity’s network applies to all user accounts that can access the network remotely, where that remote access leads to or could lead to access into the CDE. This includes all remote access by personnel (users and administrators), and third parties (including, but not limited to, vendors, suppliers, service providers, and customers). If remote access is to a part of the entity’s network that is properly segmented from the CDE, such that remote users cannot access or impact the CDE, MFA for remote access to that part of the network is not required. However, MFA is required for any remote access to networks with access to the CDE and is recommended for all remote access to the entity’s networks. The MFA requirements apply for all types of system components, including cloud, hosted systems, and on-premises applications, network security devices, workstations, servers, and endpoints, and includes access directly to an entity’s networks or systems as well as web-based access to an application or function.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine network and/or system configurations for remote access servers and systems to verify MFA is required in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe personnel (for example, users and administrators) and third parties connecting remotely to the network and verify that multi-factor authentication is required.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Requiring more than one type of authentication factor reduces the probability that an attacker can gain access to a system by masquerading as a legitimate user, because the attacker would need to compromise multiple authentication factors. This is especially true in environments where traditionally the single authentication factor employed was something a user knows, such as a password or passphrase.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) requires an individual to present a minimum of two of the three authentication factors specified in Requirement 8.3.1 before access is granted.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Using one factor twice (for example, using two separate passwords) is not considered multi- factor authentication.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: MFA systems are implemented as follows:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The MFA system is not susceptible to replay attacks.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: MFA systems cannot be bypassed by any users, including administrative users unless specifically documented, and authorized by management on an exception basis, for a limited time period.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: At least two different types of authentication factors are used.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Success of all authentication factors is required before access is granted.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: MFA systems are resistant to attack and strictly control any administrative overrides. Applicability Notes This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine vendor system documentation to verify that the MFA system is not susceptible to replay attacks.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine system configurations for the MFA implementation to verify it is configured in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interview responsible personnel and observe processes to verify that any requests to bypass MFA are specifically documented and authorized by management on an exception basis, for a limited time period.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe personnel logging into system components in the CDE to verify that access is granted only after all authentication factors are successful.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe personnel connecting remotely from outside the entity’s network to verify that access is granted only after all authentication factors are successful.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Poorly configured MFA systems can be bypassed by attackers. This requirement therefore addresses configuration of MFA system(s) that provide MFA for users accessing system components in the CDE.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Using one type of factor twice (for example, using two separate passwords) is not considered multi- factor authentication.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: A replay attack is when an attacker intercepts a valid transmission of data and then resends or redirects this communication for malicious purposes. In MFA implementations, replay attacks are typically used to gain unauthorized access by leveraging legitimate credentials.

parts.name: examples
parts.prose: EX: Examples of methods to help protect against replay attacks include, but are not limited to:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Unique session identifiers and session keys.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Timestamps.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Time-based, one-time passwords or passcodes.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Anti-replay mechanisms that detect and reject duplicated authentication attempts.

parts.name: further-information
parts.prose: FI: For more information about MFA systems and features, refer to the following:

parts.name: further-information
parts.prose: FI: PCI SSC’s Information Supplement: Multi-Factor Authentication

parts.name: further-information
parts.prose: FI: PCI SSC’s Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) on this topic.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: If accounts used by systems or applications can be used for interactive login, they are managed as follows:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interactive use is prevented unless needed for an exceptional circumstance.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interactive use is limited to the time needed for the exceptional circumstance.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Business justification for interactive use is documented.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interactive use is explicitly approved by management.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Individual user identity is confirmed before access to account is granted.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Every action taken is attributable to an individual user.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: When used interactively, all actions with accounts designated as system or application accounts are authorized and attributable to an individual person. Applicability Notes This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 8.6.1 Examine application and system accounts that can be used interactively and interview administrative personnel to verify that application and system accounts are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Like individual user accounts, system and application accounts require accountability and strict management to ensure they are used only for the intended purpose and are not misused.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Attackers often compromise system or application accounts to gain access to cardholder data.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Where possible, configure system and application accounts to disallow interactive login to prevent unauthorized individuals from logging in and using the account with its associated system privileges, and to limit the machines and devices on which the account can be used.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Interactive login is the ability for a person to log into a system or application account in the same manner as a normal user account. Using system and application accounts this way means there is no accountability and traceability of actions taken by the user.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Refer to Appendix G for the definition of “application and system accounts.”

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Passwords/passphrases for any application and system accounts that can be used for interactive login are not hard coded in scripts, configuration/property files, or bespoke and custom source code.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Passwords/passphrases used by application and system accounts cannot be used by unauthorized personnel. Applicability Notes Stored passwords/passphrases are required to be encrypted in accordance with PCI DSS Requirement 8.3.2. This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interview personnel and examine system development procedures to verify that processes are defined for application and system accounts that can be used for interactive login, specifying that passwords/passphrases are not hard coded in scripts, configuration/property files, or bespoke and custom source code.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine scripts, configuration/property files, and bespoke and custom source code for application and system accounts that can be used for interactive login, to verify passwords/passphrases for those accounts are not present.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Not properly protecting passwords/passphrases used by application and system accounts, especially if those accounts can be used for interactive login, increases the risk and success of unauthorized use of those privileged accounts.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Changing these values due to suspected or confirmed disclosure can be particularly difficult to implement.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Tools can facilitate both management and security of authentication factors for application and system accounts. For example, consider password vaults or other system-managed controls.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Passwords/passphrases for any application and system accounts are protected against misuse as follows:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Passwords/passphrases are changed periodically (at the frequency defined in the entity’s targeted risk analysis, which is performed according to all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1) and upon suspicion or confirmation of compromise.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Passwords/passphrases are constructed with sufficient complexity appropriate for how frequently the entity changes the passwords/passphrases.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Passwords/passphrases used by application and system accounts cannot be used indefinitely and are structured to resist brute-force and guessing attacks. Applicability Notes This requirement is a best practice until 31 March 2025, after which it will be required and must be fully considered during a PCI DSS assessment. 8.6.3 (continued)

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine policies and procedures to verify that procedures are defined to protect passwords/passphrases for application or system accounts against misuse in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine the entity’s targeted risk analysis for the change frequency and complexity for passwords/passphrases for application and system accounts to verify the risk analysis was performed in accordance with all elements specified in Requirement 12.3.1 and addresses:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The frequency defined for periodic changes to application and system passwords/passphrases.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The complexity defined for passwords/passphrases and appropriateness of the complexity relative to the frequency of changes.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interview responsible personnel and examine system configuration settings to verify that passwords/passphrases for any application and system accounts are protected against misuse in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Systems and application accounts pose more inherent security risk than user accounts because they often run in an elevated security context, with access to systems that may not be typically granted to user accounts, such as programmatic access to databases, etc. As a result, special consideration must be made to protect passwords/passphrases used for application and system accounts.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Entities should consider the following risk factors when determining how to protect application and system passwords/passphrases against misuse:

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: All these elements affect the level of risk for application and system accounts and might impact the security of systems accessed by the system and application accounts.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Entities should correlate their selected change frequency for application and system passwords/passwords with their selected complexity for those passwords/passphrases – i.e., the complexity should be more rigorous when passwords/passphrases are changed infrequently and can be less rigorous when changed more frequently. For example, a longer change frequency is more justifiable when passwords/passphrases complexity is set to 36 alphanumeric characters with upper- and lower- case letters, numbers, and special characters.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Best practices are to consider password changes at least once a year, a password/passphrase length of at least 15 characters, and complexity for the passwords/passphrase of alphanumeric characters, with upper- and lower-case letters, and special characters.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: How securely the passwords/passphrases are stored (for example, whether they are stored in a password vault).

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Staff turnover.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The number of people with access to the authentication factor.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Whether the account can be used for interactive login.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Whether the security posture of accounts is dynamically analyzed, and real-time access to resources is automatically determined accordingly (see Requirement 8.3.9).

parts.name: further-information
parts.prose: FI: For information about variability and equivalency of password strength for passwords/passphrases of different formats, see the industry standards (for example, the current version of NIST SP 800- 63 Digital Identity Guidelines).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All security policies and operational procedures that are identified in Requirement 9 are:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Documented.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Kept up to date.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: In use.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Known to all affected parties.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Expectations, controls, and oversight for meeting activities within Requirement 9 are defined and adhered to by affected personnel. All supporting activities are repeatable, consistently applied, and conform to management’s intent.

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.1.1 Examine documentation and interview personnel to verify that security policies and operational procedures identified in Requirement 9 are managed in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Requirement 9.1.1 is about effectively managing and maintaining the various policies and procedures specified throughout Requirement 9. While it is important to define the specific policies or procedures called out in Requirement 9, it is equally important to ensure they are properly documented, maintained, and disseminated.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: It is important to update policies and procedures as needed to address changes in processes, technologies, and business objectives. For this reason, consider updating these documents as soon as possible after a change occurs and not only on a periodic cycle.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Security policies define the entity’s security objectives and principles. Operational procedures describe how to perform activities, and define the controls, methods, and processes that are followed to achieve the desired result in a consistent manner and in accordance with policy objectives.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Policies and procedures, including updates, are actively communicated to all affected personnel, and are supported by operating procedures describing how to perform activities.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 9 are documented, assigned, and understood.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Day-to-day responsibilities for performing all the activities in Requirement 9 are allocated. Personnel are accountable for successful, continuous operation of these requirements.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine documentation to verify that descriptions of roles and responsibilities for performing activities in Requirement 9 are documented and assigned.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interview personnel with responsibility for performing activities in Requirement 9 to verify that roles and responsibilities are assigned as documented and are understood.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: If roles and responsibilities are not formally assigned, personnel may not be aware of their day-to-day responsibilities, and critical activities may not occur.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Roles and responsibilities may be documented within policies and procedures or maintained within separate documents.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: As part of communicating roles and responsibilities, entities can consider having personnel acknowledge their acceptance and understanding of their assigned roles and responsibilities.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: A method to document roles and responsibilities is a responsibility assignment matrix that includes who is responsible, accountable, consulted, and informed (also called a RACI matrix).

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Appropriate facility entry controls are in place to restrict physical access to systems in the CDE.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: System components in the CDE cannot be physically accessed by unauthorized personnel. Applicability Notes This requirement does not apply to locations that are publicly accessible by consumers (cardholders).

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.2.1 Observe entry controls and interview responsible personnel to verify that physical security controls are in place to restrict access to systems in the CDE.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Without physical access controls, unauthorized persons could potentially gain access to the CDE and sensitive information, or could alter system configurations, introduce vulnerabilities into the network, or destroy or steal equipment. Therefore, the purpose of this requirement is that physical access to the CDE is controlled via physical security controls such as badge readers or other mechanisms such as lock and key.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Whichever mechanism meets this requirement, it must be sufficient for the organization to verify that only authorized personnel are granted access.

parts.name: examples
parts.prose: EX: Facility entry controls include physical security controls at each computer room, data center, and other physical areas with systems in the CDE. It can also include badge readers or other devices that manage physical access controls, such as lock and key with a current list of all individuals holding the keys.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Physical and/or logical controls are implemented to restrict use of publicly accessible network jacks within the facility.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Unauthorized devices cannot connect to the entity’s network from public areas within the facility.

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.2.2 Interview responsible personnel and observe locations of publicly accessible network jacks to verify that physical and/or logical controls are in place to restrict access to publicly accessible network jacks within the facility.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Restricting access to network jacks (or network ports) will prevent malicious individuals from plugging into readily available network jacks and gaining access to the CDE or systems connected to the CDE.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Whether logical or physical controls, or a combination of both, are used, they should prevent an individual or device that is not explicitly authorized from being able to connect to the network.

parts.name: examples
parts.prose: EX: Methods to meet this requirement include network jacks located in public areas and areas accessible to visitors could be disabled and only enabled when network access is explicitly authorized. Alternatively, processes could be implemented to ensure that visitors are escorted at all times in areas with active network jacks.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Physical access to wireless access points, gateways, networking/communications hardware, and telecommunication lines within the facility is restricted.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Physical networking equipment cannot be accessed by unauthorized personnel.

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.2.3 Interview responsible personnel and observe locations of hardware and lines to verify that physical access to wireless access points, gateways, networking/communications hardware, and telecommunication lines within the facility is restricted.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Without appropriate physical security over access to wireless components and devices, and computer networking and telecommunications equipment and lines, malicious users could gain access to the entity’s network resources. Additionally, they could connect their own devices to the network to gain unauthorized access to the CDE or systems connected to the CDE.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Additionally, securing networking and communications hardware prevents malicious users from intercepting network traffic or physically connecting their own devices to wired network resources.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Access to consoles in sensitive areas is restricted via locking when not in use.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Physical consoles within sensitive areas cannot be used by unauthorized personnel.

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.2.4 Observe a system administrator’s attempt to log into consoles in sensitive areas and verify that they are “locked” to prevent unauthorized use.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Locking console login screens prevents unauthorized persons from gaining access to sensitive information, altering system configurations, introducing vulnerabilities into the network, or destroying records.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Procedures are implemented for authorizing and managing physical access of personnel to the CDE, including:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Identifying personnel.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Managing changes to an individual’s physical access requirements.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Revoking or terminating personnel identification.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Limiting access to the identification process or system to authorized personnel.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Requirements for access to the physical CDE are defined and enforced to identify and authorize personnel.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine documented procedures to verify that procedures to authorize and manage physical access of personnel to the CDE are defined in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe identification methods, such as ID badges, and processes to verify that personnel in the CDE are clearly identified.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe processes to verify that access to the identification process, such as a badge system, is limited to authorized personnel.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Establishing procedures for granting, managing, and removing access when it is no longer needed ensures non-authorized individuals are prevented from gaining access to areas containing cardholder data. In addition, it is important to limit access to the actual badging system and badging materials to prevent unauthorized personnel from making their own badges and/or setting up their own access rules.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: It is important to visually identify the personnel that are physically present, and whether the individual is a visitor or an employee.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Refer to Appendix G for the definition of “personnel.”

parts.name: examples
parts.prose: EX: One way to identify personnel is to assign them badges.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Procedures are implemented for authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE, including:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Visitors are authorized before entering.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Visitors are escorted at all times.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Visitors are clearly identified and given a badge or other identification that expires.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Visitor badges or other identification visibly distinguishes visitors from personnel.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Requirements for visitor access to the CDE are defined and enforced. Visitors cannot exceed any authorized physical access allowed while in the CDE.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine documented procedures and interview personnel to verify procedures are defined for authorizing and managing visitor access to the CDE in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe processes when visitors are present in the CDE and interview personnel to verify that visitors are:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Authorized before entering the CDE.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Escorted at all times within the CDE.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe the use of visitor badges or other identification to verify that the badge or other identification does not permit unescorted access to the CDE.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe visitors in the CDE to verify that:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Visitor badges or other identification are being used for all visitors.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Visitor badges or identification easily distinguish visitors from personnel.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine visitor badges or other identification and observe evidence in the badging system to verify visitor badges or other identification expires.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Visitor controls are important to reduce the ability of unauthorized and malicious persons to gain access to facilities and potentially to cardholder data.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Visitor controls ensure visitors are identifiable as visitors so personnel can monitor their activities, and that their access is restricted to just the duration of their legitimate visit.

parts.name: definitions
parts.prose: DEF: Refer to Appendix G for the definition of “visitor.”

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Visitor badges or identification are surrendered or deactivated before visitors leave the facility or at the date of expiration.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Visitor identification or badges cannot be reused after expiration.

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.3.3 Observe visitors leaving the facility and interview personnel to verify visitor badges or other identification are surrendered or deactivated before visitors leave the facility or at the date of expiration. upon departure or expiration.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Ensuring that visitor badges are returned or deactivated upon expiry or completion of the visit prevents malicious persons from using a previously authorized pass to gain physical access into the building after the visit has ended.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Visitor logs are used to maintain a physical record of visitor activity both within the facility and within sensitive areas, including:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The visitor’s name and the organization represented.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The date and time of the visit.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The name of the personnel authorizing physical access.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Retaining the log for at least three months, unless otherwise restricted by law.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Records of visitor access that enable the identification of individuals are maintained.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine the visitor logs and interview responsible personnel to verify that visitor logs are used to record physical access to both the facility and sensitive areas.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine the visitor logs and verify that the logs contain:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The visitor’s name and the organization represented.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The personnel authorizing physical access.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Date and time of visit.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine visitor log storage locations and interview responsible personnel to verify that the log is retained for at least three months, unless otherwise restricted by law.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: A visitor log documenting minimum information about the visitor is easy and inexpensive to maintain. It will assist in identifying historical physical access to a building or room and potential access to cardholder data.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: When logging the date and time of visit, including both in and out times is considered a best practice, since it provides helpful tracking information and provides assurance that a visitor has left at the end of the day. It is also good to verify that a visitor’s ID (driver’s license, etc.) matches the name they put on the visitor log.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All media with cardholder data is physically secured.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Media with cardholder data cannot be accessed by unauthorized personnel.

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.4.1. Examine documentation to verify that the procedures defined for protecting cardholder data include controls for physically securing all media.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Controls for physically securing media are intended to prevent unauthorized persons from gaining access to cardholder data on any media. Cardholder data is susceptible to unauthorized viewing, copying, or scanning if it is unprotected while it is on removable or portable media, printed out, or left on someone’s desk.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: All media with cardholder data is classified in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Media are classified and protected appropriately.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined for classifying media with cardholder data in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine media logs or other documentation to verify that all media is classified in accordance with the sensitivity of the data.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Media not identified as confidential may not be adequately protected or may be lost or stolen.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: It is important that media be identified such that its classification status is apparent. This does not mean however that the media needs to have a “confidential” label.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Media with cardholder data sent outside the facility is secured as follows:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Media sent outside the facility is logged.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Media is sent by secured courier or other delivery method that can be accurately tracked.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Offsite tracking logs include details about media location.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Media is secured and tracked when transported outside the facility.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined for securing media sent outside the facility in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Interview personnel and examine records to verify that all media sent outside the facility is logged and sent via secured courier or other delivery method that can be tracked.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine offsite tracking logs for all media to verify tracking details are documented.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Media may be lost or stolen if sent via a non- trackable method such as regular postal mail. The use of secure couriers to deliver any media that contains cardholder data allows organizations to use their tracking systems to maintain inventory and location of shipments.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Management approves all media with cardholder data that is moved outside the facility (including when media is distributed to individuals).

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Media cannot leave a facility without the approval of accountable personnel. Applicability Notes Individuals approving media movements should have the appropriate level of management authority to grant this approval. However, it is not specifically required that such individuals have “manager” as part of their title.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined to ensure that media moved outside the facility is approved by management.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine offsite media tracking logs and interview responsible personnel to verify that proper management authorization is obtained for all media moved outside the facility (including media distributed to individuals).

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Without a firm process for ensuring that all media movements are approved before the media is removed from secure areas, the media would not be tracked or appropriately protected, and its location would be unknown, leading to lost or stolen media.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Inventory logs of all electronic media with cardholder data are maintained.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Accurate inventories of stored electronic media are maintained.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine documentation to verify that procedures are defined to maintain electronic media inventory logs.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine electronic media inventory logs and interview responsible personnel to verify that logs are maintained.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Without careful inventory methods and storage controls, stolen or missing electronic media could go unnoticed for an indefinite amount of time.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Hard-copy materials with cardholder data are destroyed when no longer needed for business or legal reasons, as follows:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Materials are cross-cut shredded, incinerated, or pulped so that cardholder data cannot be reconstructed.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Materials are stored in secure storage containers prior to destruction.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Cardholder data cannot be recovered from media that has been destroyed or which is pending destruction. Applicability Notes These requirements for media destruction when that media is no longer needed for business or legal reasons are separate and distinct from PCI DSS Requirement 3.2.1, which is for securely deleting cardholder data when no longer needed per the entity’s cardholder data retention policies.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine the media destruction policy to verify that procedures are defined to destroy hard- copy media with cardholder data when no longer needed for business or legal reasons in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe processes and interview personnel to verify that hard-copy materials are cross-cut shredded, incinerated, or pulped such that cardholder data cannot be reconstructed.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe storage containers used for materials that contain information to be destroyed to verify that the containers are secure.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: If steps are not taken to destroy information contained on hard-copy media before disposal, malicious individuals may retrieve information from the disposed media, leading to a data compromise. For example, malicious individuals may use a technique known as “dumpster diving,” where they search through trashcans and recycle bins looking for hard-copy materials with information they can use to launch an attack.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Securing storage containers used for materials that are going to be destroyed prevents sensitive information from being captured while the materials are being collected.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Consider “to-be-shredded” containers with a lock that prevents access to its contents or that physically prevent access to the inside of the container.

parts.name: further-information
parts.prose: FI: See NIST Special Publication 800-88, Revision 1: Guidelines for Media Sanitization.

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: Electronic media with cardholder data is destroyed when no longer needed for business or legal reasons via one of the following:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The electronic media is destroyed.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: The cardholder data is rendered unrecoverable so that it cannot be reconstructed.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: Cardholder data cannot be recovered from media that has been erased or destroyed. Applicability Notes These requirements for media destruction when that media is no longer needed for business or legal reasons are separate and distinct from PCI DSS Requirement 3.2.1, which is for securely deleting cardholder data when no longer needed per the entity’s cardholder data retention policies.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Examine the media destruction policy to verify that procedures are defined to destroy electronic media when no longer needed for business or legal reasons in accordance with all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Observe the media destruction process and interview responsible personnel to verify that electronic media with cardholder data is destroyed via one of the methods specified in this requirement.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: If steps are not taken to destroy information contained on electronic media when no longer needed, malicious individuals may retrieve information from the disposed media, leading to a data compromise. For example, malicious individuals may use a technique known as “dumpster diving,” where they search through trashcans and recycle bins looking for information they can use to launch an attack.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: The deletion function in most operating systems allows deleted data to be recovered, so instead, a dedicated secure deletion function or application should be used to make data unrecoverable.

parts.name: examples
parts.prose: EX: Methods for securely destroying electronic media include secure wiping in accordance with industry-accepted standards for secure deletion, degaussing, or physical destruction (such as grinding or shredding hard disks).

parts.name: further-information
parts.prose: FI: See NIST Special Publication 800-88, Revision 1: Guidelines for Media Sanitization

parts.name: statement
parts.prose: POI devices that capture payment card data via direct physical interaction with the payment card form factor are protected from tampering and unauthorized substitution, including the following:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Maintaining a list of POI devices.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Periodically inspecting POI devices to look for tampering or unauthorized substitution.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Training personnel to be aware of suspicious behavior and to report tampering or unauthorized substitution of devices.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Components used only for manual PAN key entry.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) devices (for example, smartphones or tablets), which are mobile merchant-owned devices designed for mass-market distribution.

parts.name: objective
parts.prose: The entity has defined procedures to protect and manage point-of-interaction devices. Expectations, controls, and oversight for the management and protection of POI devices are defined and adhered to by affected personnel. Applicability Notes These requirements apply to deployed POI devices used in card-present transactions (that is, a payment card form factor such as a card that is swiped, tapped, or dipped). These requirements do not apply to:

parts.name: assessment-method
parts.prose: 9.5.1 Examine documented policies and procedures to verify that processes are defined that include all elements specified in this requirement.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: Criminals attempt to steal payment card data by stealing and/or manipulating card-reading devices and terminals. Criminals will try to steal devices so they can learn how to break into them, and they often try to replace legitimate devices with fraudulent devices that send them payment card data every time a card is entered.

parts.name: purpose
parts.prose: They will also try to add “skimming” components to the outside of devices, which are designed to capture payment card data before it enters the device—for example, by attaching an additional card reader on top of the legitimate card reader so that the payment card data is captured twice: once by the criminal’s component and then by the device’s legitimate component. In this way, transactions may still be completed without interruption while the criminal is “skimming” the payment card data during the process.

parts.name: good-practice
parts.prose: GP: Entities may consider implementing protection from tampering and unauthorized substitution for:

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Components used only for manual PAN key entry.

parts.name: item
parts.prose: Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) devices (for example, smartphones or tablets), which are mobile merchant-owned devices designed for mass-market distribution.

parts.name: further-information
parts.prose: FI: Additional best practices on skimming prevention are available on the PCI SSC website.
